summary: Stancil argues that Fivetran’s dominance is not inevitable despite riding two waves: SaaS proliferation and centralized data warehouses. He lays out three ways Fivetran could fail—if the need to move data fades, if SaaS vendors and warehouses change how data is moved, or if a better/cheaper tool displaces it—while ultimately betting on inertia but acknowledging how quickly the “impractical” can become the new normal.
money_quotes: Fivetran is, if nothing else, a product of its time.
We’d all be better off—well, all of us except ELT vendors—if more apps did what Segment does, and wrote data straight to the warehouse themselves.
This, to me, is the biggest structural risk to Fivetran. Apps build outlet stores, and go DTC. SaaS vendors giveth Fivetran its market; SaaS vendors could taketh it away.
I’m skeptical of this too. The data market is moving aggressively towards hosted services.
Sometimes, the future that looks unreasonable and impractical today ends up looking right and natural tomorrow.
key_insight: Fivetran’s position is strong but structurally vulnerable if SaaS vendors and warehouses shift data movement upstream, eroding the economics that make ELT a standalone business.
